module_name: Smart Probes and Air Data Systems
why_this_matters: Air data is the pilot's connection to the physics of flight. Airspeed, altitude, angle of attack, vertical speed, Mach number, and temperature are not cockpit conveniences. They are the numbers that tell the crew whether the wing is flying or stalling, whether the airframe is at risk of overspeed, and whether the aircraft is at the altitude ATC cleared it to. When those numbers are wrong, the autopilot follows them, the flight directors command to them, and the crew reacts to them. An air data failure is never a benign write-up. It is the beginning of a chain that, if uninterrupted, ends in an accident.

The history is explicit. Air France 447 (2009, Atlantic): iced pitot probes gave the Airbus A330 unreliable airspeed for 29 seconds; the autopilot disconnected, the crew mishandled manual flight at altitude, and 228 people died in the South Atlantic. Aeroperu 603 (1996, Lima): masking tape left over the static ports during a wash caused the altimeters and airspeed indicators to read garbage for the entire climb; the crew flew into the Pacific at night at cruise power, unable to tell climbing from descending. Birgenair 301 (1996, Puerto Plata): a pitot tube left uncovered on the ramp for weeks had a mud-dauber nest built inside it; the captain's airspeed indication was frozen near rotation speed during climb, the autopilot trimmed the aircraft nose-up to hold the false speed, and the aircraft stalled into the Atlantic. XL Airways 888T (2008, Perpignan): an Airbus A320 on an acceptance flight lost both AoA vanes to ice-contamination residue from a prior ground wash; the alpha-protection system reverted, the crew exceeded the smooth-stall envelope at low altitude, and seven people died.

Smart probes and modern air data computers embed computation where there used to be a column of mercury and a length of rubber hose. That is mostly good: the digital output is more accurate, the common-mode failures are better managed, and the self-test catches things the old systems could not. But when a smart probe is wrong, it is wrong with full digital confidence, and it pushes that wrong value into the autopilot at 50 or 100 times per second. Your job is not to trust the probe because it is smart. Your job is to verify heating, drainage, pneumatic integrity, calibration, and digital output with the same discipline a craftsperson applied to a rubber hose in 1960 — and then some, because now an embedded processor can lie faster than a human can catch it.

key_terms.definition: A pitot-static (and sometimes AoA and TAT) sensor assembly with an integrated processor that converts analog pressures into calibrated digital output at the probe. Replaces pneumatic plumbing from probe to cockpit with a digital data bus (typically ARINC 429). Examples: Rosemount 0858, Thales Multi-Function Probe on A350.

A smart probe that integrates pitot pressure, static pressure, AoA vane input, and sometimes TAT into a single externally-mounted unit. Reduces skin penetrations from 6–9 to 2–3 and eliminates many shared-source failure paths, but introduces new ones (a single MFP failure takes multiple parameters with it).

A vane AoA sensor is a small aerodynamic flag that rotates on a shaft to align with the relative wind; position is read by an RVDT or resolver. Smart AoA uses multiple static pressure taps around a hemispherical or cylindrical probe and calculates AoA from pressure differentials with no moving parts (example: Rosemount 858AJ). Smart AoA eliminates vane freeze-up but requires cleaner aerodynamic installation.

Total Air Temperature probe. A heated, forward-facing probe that measures the temperature of air brought to rest against a platinum resistance thermometer. Reads higher than static air temperature due to compressibility heating of the incoming flow. Must be heated to prevent icing but with low enough bias to avoid cooking the sensing element on the ground.

key_terms.term: Total Air Temp (TAT) vs Static Air Temp (SAT)
key_terms.definition: SAT is the actual ambient temperature of still air at altitude. TAT is the temperature measured by a probe in the moving airstream, including the kinetic (ram-rise) heating. The relationship: TAT = SAT × (1 + 0.2 × M² × Kr), where M is Mach and Kr is the recovery factor (typically 0.95–1.00). At M0.8 the difference is approximately 40 °C — SAT of −55 °C yields TAT of about −15 °C.

Electrical resistance heating embedded in the pitot tube (and often the static port, AoA vane, and TAT) to prevent ice formation from blocking the sensor. Typically draws 5–15 A at 28 V DC per probe on turbine aircraft. Controlled by a pitot heat control unit that can modulate power on the ground (low heat, preserve probe life) vs. in flight (high heat, prevent ice).

Mach = √[5 × ((qc/p + 1)^(2/7) − 1)] for subsonic flow, where qc is impact pressure (pt − ps) and p is static pressure. The ADC computes Mach in real time from pitot and static inputs, then feeds it to Vmo/Mmo warnings, autothrottle, and Mach trim. Pitot or static errors propagate directly into Mach error.

A computer that receives pitot, static, AoA, and TAT inputs (either as analog pressures through pneumatic lines or as digital data from smart probes) and outputs calibrated altitude, airspeed (IAS/CAS/EAS/TAS), Mach, vertical speed, and air temperature on a digital bus (usually ARINC 429). Feeds PFD, autopilot, autothrottle, FMS, transponder, and FDR.

The ARINC 429 labels that carry air data between the ADC and consumers. Label 203 = altitude (BCD), 204 = baro-corrected altitude, 205 = Mach, 206 = computed airspeed (CAS), 210 = true airspeed, 211 = total air temperature, 212 = static air temperature, 221 = AoA. Rate: typically 16–20 Hz. Bus speed: low-speed 12.5 kbps or high-speed 100 kbps.

The static air temperature the ADC reports to the cockpit, derived from TAT after correcting for ram-rise heating using Mach and recovery factor. On the ground OAT ≈ TAT because the aircraft is stationary. At M0.8 OAT is computed by subtracting the ram rise.

Four airspeeds, each with a specific meaning. IAS (Indicated) = raw pitot-static pressure converted to airspeed. CAS (Calibrated) = IAS corrected for instrument and position error (what the ADC actually displays on the PFD). EAS (Equivalent) = CAS corrected for compressibility (used in structural loads). TAS (True) = CAS corrected for air density (used for navigation, range, and fuel burn). The ADC computes all four internally.

The diagram of how pitot and static pressure lines are routed from probes through drains, splits, and ADCs to cockpit instruments. Critical for failure analysis — a single static line blockage may drive three altimeters and two transponders to a common wrong value. Modern aircraft with smart probes have a degenerate pitot-static tree (one probe = one digital bus), which changes the failure modes.

Pitot heat elements are resistive, and the resistance at ambient temperature is a direct check of element health. A Rosemount 0851HT nominal cold resistance is approximately 2 Ω for a 14 A heater at 28 V (P = V²/R, 28²/2 = 392 W per probe). A reading 20% above nominal indicates a broken internal conductor strand or a cracked element; a reading near zero indicates a short to case.

Built-In Test Equipment and Continuous Built-In Test. The self-diagnostic capability inside a smart probe or ADC that continuously monitors sensor health, heating, and computation validity and reports faults as ARINC 429 SSM (Sign-Status Matrix) codes or on a maintenance bus. PBIT (Power-up BIT) runs at power on; CBIT runs continuously; IBIT (Initiated BIT) runs on command during maintenance.

The calibration curve inside the ADC that corrects raw pitot-static pressure for the aerodynamic position of the probe on the airframe. Different probe locations see slightly different local static pressure due to aircraft flow field. PEC is AoA- and Mach-dependent and is a unique calibration per airframe type — never swap a Cessna ADC into a Hawker.

Air Data Computer (ADC) Architecture.title: Air Data Computer Architecture
Air Data Computer (ADC) Architecture.overview: An ADC is a sealed, ARINC-form-factor computer (typically 2 MCU or 4 MCU) that replaces what used to be a rack of individual aneroid-and-bellows instruments. It ingests pneumatic pressures (from conventional probes) or digital data (from smart probes), applies calibration and filtering, computes every airspeed and altitude parameter the aircraft needs, and broadcasts them on a data bus at a steady rate. It is not just a sensor; it is a high-integrity computation engine with triple-redundant arithmetic and continuous self-check.
key_points: Inputs: pitot pressure (Pt), static pressure (Ps), AoA (local α), total air temperature (Tt), and a baro-correction setting from the cockpit (29.92 inHg for flight levels, local altimeter setting below transition altitude).

Sensors: precision vibrating-cylinder or silicon-resonant pressure transducers with resolution of approximately 0.01 inHg (about 10 feet at sea level) and drift of < 1 foot per month. These are the most expensive components in the box.

Computation: altitude from Ps via ICAO standard atmosphere, CAS from (Pt − Ps) via the St.-Venant–Wantzel compressible flow equation, Mach from qc/Ps, TAS from CAS and air density, vertical speed by numerical differentiation of altitude with a 1-second smoothing filter.

Outputs: ARINC 429 high-speed or low-speed bus, typically two redundant output buses per ADC. Labels 203, 204, 205, 206, 210, 211, 212, 221. Rates 16–20 Hz. SSM field carries validity — 'Normal Operation', 'No Computed Data', 'Functional Test', or 'Failure Warning'.

Certified under TSO-C106 (Air Data Computer) with compliance to ARINC 706 performance standard. Individual function TSOs stack on top: TSO-C2d covers airspeed indicator function, TSO-C10b covers altimeter function, TSO-C44c covers AoA, TSO-C43c covers total temperature indicator.

Typical part numbers: Honeywell AZ-900-series ADCs in most Boeing narrowbody and Gulfstream aircraft, Collins AHC-3000 and ADC-3000 in Challenger/Global/Legacy jets, Thales IESI-2020 in Airbus A350/A380.

Redundancy: two ADCs minimum (captain's and first officer's). RVSM-approved aircraft require dual independent ADCs with independent pitot-static sources. Three-ADC installations are common on widebodies with a center standby ADC fed by an independent standby pitot-static system.

14 CFR 91.411 requires altimeter and static-system testing every 24 calendar months on any aircraft flown under IFR. The test verifies ADC computation accuracy against a calibrated test set across the altitude band the aircraft operates in.

Smart Probes.title: Smart Probes
Smart Probes.overview: A smart probe is a pitot or multi-function probe with an embedded ARINC 429 transmitter. Instead of pneumatic tubing running 30–50 feet from the probe to the ADC in the avionics bay, a twisted-shielded pair runs from the probe to a Remote Data Concentrator or directly to the consumer. The analog-to-digital conversion happens at the probe head, where pneumatic lag and condensation are minimized and where the processor can continuously monitor sensor health.
key_points: Common products: Rosemount 0858 Smart Probe (Boeing 737NG/MAX standby system, Gulfstream G-series), Rosemount 858AJ multi-function probe (newer narrowbody applications with integrated AoA), Thales MFP (Airbus A350, A380).

Internal architecture: dual pressure transducers (pitot and static ports on the same probe body), an RVDT or strain-gage for AoA if multi-function, a platinum RTD for TAT if equipped, an ARM or PowerPC processor running certified software, ARINC 429 transmitter, and an integrated heater.

Advantages over pneumatic plumbing: eliminates pneumatic lag (typically 20–200 ms on long runs), eliminates condensation traps and drain points along the pneumatic path, eliminates many shared-source failure modes in which a single static line blockage affects multiple consumers.

New failure modes introduced: single-point hardware failure of the probe takes multiple parameters with it (pitot + static + AoA from one box). Mitigated by installing 3 probes on opposite sides and voting (Airbus A350 uses 3 MFPs; the FMS votes on best-estimate).

Heating on smart probes is more aggressive than on analog probes because the electronics are co-located. Ground heating is modulated to avoid cooking the internals; in-flight heating is full-power. Typical power draw: 15 A at 28 V for the probe heater plus 1–2 A for the electronics.

Smart probes carry their own TSO — an MFP is typically certified under TSO-C16a (pitot tubes) combined with TSO-C106 (air data) and TSO-C44c (AoA) when applicable. The combined unit must pass all three TSO environmental qualifications.

Calibration is factory-stored in non-volatile memory per serial number. Swapping an MFP between airframes of the same model is normally permitted, but the aircraft-specific position-error correction may need to be re-programmed via the maintenance bus (ARINC 738 service port) per the IPC/AMM.

Angle of Attack (AOA) Measurement.title: Angle of Attack Measurement
Angle of Attack (AOA) Measurement.overview: AoA is the angle between the wing chord and the relative wind. It is the parameter that determines whether a wing is lifting or stalling, and it is independent of airspeed, weight, configuration, or bank angle. A wing that is flying at 15 degrees AoA is approaching stall whether it is doing so at 120 knots and a full-gross takeoff or at 280 knots and a high-altitude maneuver. Measuring it correctly is the single most important input to every modern stall protection system.
key_points: Vane sensors (legacy and many current aircraft): a small aerodynamic vane on a heated shaft aligns with the relative wind, and an RVDT or resolver reads shaft position. Heated to 28 V DC to prevent the shaft from freezing. The vane must be symmetric and balanced; any ice accumulation or residue from a ground wash (see XL Airways 888T) biases it.

Smart AoA / pressure-based AoA: multiple static pressure taps around a hemispherical or cylindrical probe; the pressure differential vs angle is calibrated into a table. No moving parts. Examples: Rosemount 858AJ, Collins AoA probe on Challenger 350, Aerosonic APS-400.
Outputs: an analog signal (AC synchro on legacy aircraft, 0–10 V DC on some), or ARINC 429 label 221 on digital systems. Sample rate 16–40 Hz for stall-protection timing.

Integration with stall protection: AoA drives the stick shaker (low-speed warning at stall AoA − 1 to 2 degrees), the stick pusher (if fitted, at stall AoA), the auto-slat/flap extension on some aircraft, and the alpha-floor autothrottle mode on Airbus fly-by-wire.

TSO-C44c covers AoA sensors. Environmental qualification includes altitude, temperature cycling, ice accretion, and vibration. RTCA DO-200 (processing of aeronautical data) applies to the AoA table programmed into the ADC.

Accident citation: XL Airways 888T (A320, 2008) — two AoA vanes contaminated with residue froze at descent attitude. Alpha protection failed to trigger, the crew attempted a handling check below safe altitude, and the aircraft stalled. Always verify AoA vane free movement through the full mechanical stop range during inspection.

Ground functional test: a calibrated AoA protractor or a manufacturer-specific test fixture rotates the vane (or simulates pressure for smart AoA) through known angles while the ADC output is monitored on ARINC 429. Typical tolerance: ±0.5 degrees at 5-degree increments across ±30 degrees.

RVSM (Reduced Vertical Separation Minimu.title: RVSM Airspace and Air Data Accuracy
RVSM (Reduced Vertical Separation Minimu.overview: RVSM allows 1,000-foot vertical separation between FL290 and FL410 instead of the 2,000-foot separation used at lower altitudes. It doubles the usable cruise altitudes in the high-altitude airspace structure and saves billions in fuel annually. It works only because participating aircraft maintain extraordinarily accurate altitude measurement and altitude-hold. An RVSM failure is not a pilot-cert question — it is a system-integrity question answered by the avionics technician.
key_points: Equipment required: two independent primary altitude sources (typically two ADCs, each with its own pitot-static source), one altitude-hold autopilot, one altitude alerting system, one Mode-S or Mode-C altitude-reporting transponder.

Tolerance: the RVSM height-keeping performance standard calls for total vertical error (TVE) ≤ 245 feet at FL350, with altimetry system error (ASE) being the dominant contributor at approximately ±65 feet. That is equivalent to an ADC maintaining static pressure accuracy of ~0.08 inHg.

14 CFR 91 Subpart H / AC 91-85 governs RVSM authorization. Initial approval requires coordinated testing of the entire pitot-static and ADC chain against a master test stand with traceability to NIST; biennial re-verification is required.

Height monitoring: aircraft flying in RVSM airspace are periodically sampled by ground-based Height Monitoring Units (HMU) that compare reported Mode-S altitude to GPS-derived geometric altitude corrected for ISA. Repeat offenders lose RVSM approval.

Common failure drivers: a static port that is slightly deformed by a bird strike or hail (changes position error by > 100 feet at FL390), an ADC drift that accumulates over time, a static line with a slow drain-trap leak that only manifests at low differential pressure (i.e., cruise altitude).

After any work on the pitot-static system or ADC in an RVSM-approved aircraft, the aircraft is out of RVSM approval until re-tested per AC 91-85. Document in the logbook: 'RVSM-critical maintenance performed. Aircraft removed from RVSM until 91.411/91.413 test completed.' No exceptions.

RVSM suspension happens immediately if the two ADCs diverge by more than 200 feet at cruise, or if a single ADC reports an internal BITE failure that affects altitude computation. The crew coordinates with ATC for a non-RVSM altitude.

Key Exam Topics (15 Questions).title: Key Exam Topics
Key Exam Topics (15 Questions).overview: The written exam on this module is blueprint-weighted to the practical failures you will investigate in the hangar. It is not a memorization test. Each topic below maps to a specific class of maintenance action you will perform when an air data write-up lands on the shop floor.
key_points: ADC architecture: know the inputs (Pt, Ps, α, Tt, baro-setting), the outputs (ARINC 429 labels 203, 204, 205, 206, 210, 211, 212, 221), and the rate (16–20 Hz).
Smart probe advantages: elimination of pneumatic lag, condensation-trap drainage, and shared-line failure modes; plus disadvantages of single-point probe failures affecting multiple parameters.
AoA sensor types: vane with RVDT/resolver vs. multi-port pressure-based smart AoA; stall-protection integration with stick shaker, pusher, and auto-throttle alpha-floor.
RVSM requirements: two independent ADCs, 24-month re-cert, ±65-foot ASE at FL350, height monitoring units.
Shared-source failure analysis: why one blocked static port can kill three altimeters and two transponders; why AF 447 (common-mode pitot icing) affected all three ADCs.
ADC self-test: PBIT at power-on, CBIT continuous, IBIT on maintenance command; SSM field decoding on ARINC 429.
Redundancy architectures: dual ADC, triple MFP with voting, standby ADC with independent pitot-static.
Temperature measurement: TAT probe with platinum RTD, ram-rise correction to SAT, recovery factor ~0.95–1.00, typical TAT–SAT difference of 40 °C at M0.8.
Mach derivation: Mach = f(qc/Ps), why a static error corrupts both Mach and CAS, Vmo vs Mmo crossover altitude.
TSO standards: TSO-C2d airspeed, TSO-C10b altimeter, TSO-C16a pitot probes, TSO-C44c AoA, TSO-C106 ADC.

synthesized_tasks.heading: Task 1 — Pitot-Static Leak Check
synthesized_tasks.body: Perform a pitot-static leak check per 14 CFR 91.411 and the aircraft AMM. Apply a calibrated vacuum/pressure to the static and pitot systems, verify no leakage exceeds the AMM specification (typically ≤ 100 feet of altitude drop in 1 minute when tested at an equivalent altitude of 25,000 feet, and ≤ 5 knots of airspeed drop in 1 minute at 100 knots indicated). Identify leak sources when they occur and repair per approved data. Document pass/fail on AC Form 8130-3 or the equivalent maintenance record.

synthesized_tasks.heading: Task 2 — Smart Probe Functional Test
synthesized_tasks.body: Perform an end-to-end functional test of a smart probe (Rosemount 0858 or equivalent) using the aircraft maintenance computer and a calibrated pitot-static test set. Verify probe heater operation at commanded power levels, confirm ARINC 429 output labels and SSM validity, inject simulated pitot and static pressures and verify the smart probe reports altitudes and airspeeds within the AMM tolerance across the envelope. Capture BITE fault history and clear after sign-off.

synthesized_tasks.heading: Task 3 — AoA Vane Calibration
synthesized_tasks.body: Perform an AoA vane calibration on a legacy vane-type sensor using an AoA protractor or manufacturer-specific fixture. Verify the vane moves freely through the full mechanical stop range with no binding or residue. Rotate the vane to five known angles (−20°, −10°, 0°, +10°, +20°) and record the ADC ARINC 429 label 221 output at each point. Tolerance ±0.5° at each angle; if out of tolerance, follow the AMM rigging procedure or replace the vane assembly.

synthesized_tasks.heading: Task 4 — Pitot Heat System Test
synthesized_tasks.body: Perform a functional test of the pitot heat system. Measure pitot heater element resistance with the aircraft cold (typical 2 Ω nominal for a 14 A heater on 28 V; tolerance ±20%). Energize the pitot heat with the aircraft on external power and measure current draw against the AMM spec (typical 5–15 A per probe in flight mode). Verify heater operation by touch with an IR thermometer or by the manufacturer's ohmic-delta method. Confirm the cockpit annunciator logic (heat-on light extinguished, pitot-cold warning on loss of current).

synthesized_tasks.heading: Task 5 — ADC Output Verification (ARINC 429)
synthesized_tasks.body: Use a DAC ARINC 429 analyzer (Avionics Interface Technologies PBA.pro, Data Device Corporation DD-429, or equivalent) to verify the ADC's data bus output. Confirm labels 203 (altitude), 205 (Mach), 206 (CAS), 210 (TAS), 211 (TAT), 212 (SAT), 221 (AoA) are present at the specified update rate (16–20 Hz) with SSM set to 'Normal Operation'. Inject known pitot-static inputs and verify the label values match the test set reference within the AMM tolerance. Capture and decode any failure warnings or NCD indications.

synthesized_tasks.heading: Task 6 — RVSM Altimetry Recertification
synthesized_tasks.body: Perform RVSM-level altimetry testing per AC 91-85, 14 CFR 91.411, and the aircraft's RVSM Maintenance Manual (RMM). Verify altitude accuracy at multiple flight-level points (typically FL250, FL350, FL410) against a traceable master test set. Acceptance: primary altimeter accuracy within ±50 feet at FL350 and case-drain leak-rate below spec. Document the recertification and return the aircraft to RVSM status only after all primary systems pass. Any failure keeps the aircraft out of RVSM airspace until resolved.

Check.title: Pitot-Static Leak Check
step_by_step: Review the aircraft AMM leak-test procedure and note the maximum allowable leak rate for both pitot and static at the test altitudes the AMM specifies (typical: 100 ft/min at 25,000 ft altitude; 5 kt/min at 100 KIAS).
Inspect every static port and pitot head for physical damage, blockage, insect nests, or wash residue. Photograph anything unusual before applying test pressure.

Install the test set adapters on all pitot and static ports. Use manufacturer-supplied silicone adapters — not masking tape, not speed tape, not electrical tape. Bad adapters leak and give you false failures.
Cap or mask any static ports that are not connected to the system under test (e.g., alternate static source, standby static) to prevent loss of differential pressure.

Configure the test set for the system you are testing. On a Barfield DPS-450 or Laversab 6300: pressurize slowly to avoid over-speeding any instrument, never exceed the AMM maximum test altitude, and never reverse pitot/static plumbing (can blow an altimeter aneroid).

Take the system to the AMM-specified test altitude (e.g., 25,000 ft). Stabilize for 30 seconds. Record starting altitude. Isolate the test set from the system. Time 1 minute. Record ending altitude. Leak = difference.
Repeat for the pitot side: take the system to 100 KIAS or 150 KIAS per AMM, stabilize, isolate, time 1 minute, record.

If leak rate exceeds spec, pressurize to the test altitude and use a soap-bubble solution on every line joint, fitting, drain, and probe base to localize the leak. A slow hiss or growing bubble identifies the leak point.
Repair leaks per approved data — replace gaskets, re-torque fittings to AMM spec, or replace the line. Re-test after every repair.
Return the test set to zero slowly. Never dump the system; slow decompression protects altimeter diaphragms.
Remove adapters and restore the aircraft to flight configuration. Re-inspect static ports and pitot heads for cleanliness before closing out.
evaluator_watching_for: Candidate reads the AMM test-rate limits before connecting the test set — does not guess.
Manufacturer-approved adapters used on every port. Tape or improvised seals = immediate fail.
Slow pressurization and slow return to zero. Over-speed of any instrument is a finding.
Correct port isolation during the test (no leakage from an unused port that is not capped).
Leak localization with soap bubbles when a leak is detected — not hand-waving 'probably the left static line'.
RVSM-approved aircraft: candidate recognizes RVSM status is suspended until re-cert, and documents appropriately.
common_mistakes: Pressurizing too fast, blowing past the AMM altitude-rate limit (typically 3,000 ft/min maximum). Can slam an altimeter aneroid and create a new write-up bigger than the one you are chasing.
Reversing pitot and static connections on the test set. This pumps the altimeter backward — irreversible damage possible.
Leaving a static port uncapped during a pitot-side test. False leak reading.
Testing on a hot ramp without correcting for temperature. The pneumatic lines expand thermally; a leak is not what you are measuring.
Forgetting to un-isolate the test set before dumping pressure, leaving the aircraft at altitude indefinitely while the test rig bleeds to zero.
On RVSM aircraft: performing a non-RVSM-grade leak test and then not logging RVSM suspension. The aircraft flies into RVSM airspace uncertified. Regulatory and safety violation.

Test.title: Smart Probe Functional Test and Pitot Heat
step_by_step: For a smart probe functional test: review the probe's IPC page and AMM test procedure. Identify the probe part number (e.g., Rosemount 0858-1234-01), the ARINC 429 labels it transmits, and the expected signal rate.
Connect the maintenance ARINC 429 port to a portable bus analyzer. Power the aircraft per AMM (often external power on essential bus only, avionics on).

Initiate the probe's IBIT via the maintenance computer or the CMC. The probe runs PBIT internally at power-on; IBIT forces a deeper diagnostic including heater continuity check, pressure transducer self-test, and ARINC 429 loopback.
Capture BITE fault history from the probe's non-volatile memory. Review for any latent faults since the last flight.

Verify ARINC 429 output: correct labels present at correct rate, SSM set to 'Normal Operation' or 'Functional Test' as appropriate, data word parity correct, no parity errors over 60 seconds of capture.

For the pitot heat portion: with aircraft cold, measure heater element resistance at the connector pins per AMM. A Rosemount 0851HT nominal is approximately 2 Ω for a 14 A, 28 V DC heater (P = V²/R = 28²/2 = 392 W). Tolerance ±20% typical; outside tolerance indicates a broken strand or aged element.

Apply external power and energize pitot heat in ground-test mode (reduced duty cycle). Measure current draw at the circuit breaker or with a clamp-on ammeter. Typical 5–8 A per probe in ground mode, 10–15 A in flight mode.

After 30 seconds of ground-heat operation (never longer — smart probe electronics can overheat on the ground with no cooling airflow), verify the probe is warm by IR thermometer or touch. Secure pitot heat.

Confirm cockpit annunciator logic: heater on indicator illuminated during test, 'pitot cold' warning activated when heater is commanded on but current is not detected (many systems use a current-sensing relay).
Clear BITE fault history after the test is complete and all faults have been addressed. Sign off the maintenance record with the BITE capture attached.
evaluator_watching_for: Candidate identifies the correct part number and confirms the probe they are testing matches the IPC.
Aircraft powered correctly for the test (external power with avionics bus energized, not battery-only).
ARINC 429 analyzer configured for the correct speed (low-speed 12.5 kbps vs high-speed 100 kbps) and decoding the correct labels.
Cold resistance measurement taken BEFORE energizing the heater — not after, when the element is hot.
Ground-heat duration kept short. Candidate understands the smart probe can cook itself on the ground.
BITE history reviewed and addressed, not cleared without investigation.
common_mistakes: Energizing pitot heat for minutes on the ground. The probe is designed to be cooled by airstream. On the ground with no airflow, the electronics package reaches damaging temperatures in 2–3 minutes.
Measuring heater resistance after a test run when the element is hot. Resistance of the platinum heating element varies with temperature; a hot reading misleads the accept/reject call by ±10%.
Clearing BITE faults without investigating them. A latent fault today is an in-flight write-up tomorrow.
Using a general-purpose DMM for the ARINC 429 check instead of a bus analyzer. DMM cannot decode the serial labels.
On a multi-function probe: testing only the pitot function and declaring the probe good. The static and AoA functions have their own BITE and must be verified independently.

Calibration.title: AoA Vane Calibration
step_by_step: Review the AMM AoA calibration procedure for the specific aircraft type. Note the calibration angles, tolerance, and the ADC ARINC 429 label 221 scaling (typically 1 LSB = 0.05 or 0.1 degree).
Power the aircraft per the AMM. Connect a bus analyzer to the ADC maintenance port. Locate label 221 AoA and configure the analyzer to decode it in degrees.
Inspect the AoA vane for physical damage, cracks, residue from recent wash, and free movement through the full mechanical stop range. Any binding, even slight, is a reject condition.

Install the AoA calibration fixture. The most common is an AoA protractor — a bubble-level or inclinometer bracket that clamps to the vane and reads mechanical angle directly. Manufacturer-specific fixtures exist for pressure-based smart AoA and inject known pressure differentials.
Rotate the vane to the first test angle (typically −20°) and hold. Read the ARINC 429 label 221 output on the analyzer. Record both mechanical angle and reported angle.
Repeat for angles −10°, 0°, +10°, +20°. Some procedures add ±30° to test the full range.
Calculate the error at each point. Tolerance typical: ±0.5° across the primary range, ±1.0° at the ends.

If out of tolerance at any point: check the mechanical rigging (vane-to-shaft alignment), inspect the RVDT or resolver output directly (shaft angle vs electrical output), check for wear in the bearing. Re-rig per AMM or replace the assembly.
After any re-rig or replacement, re-run the entire calibration — not just the failed point. Rigging a vane to pass one angle can throw another out of tolerance.

Remove the calibration fixture. Verify the vane swings freely and returns to neutral. Close out per AMM including any post-test functional check of stall-warning system (stick shaker activation at the correct AoA).
evaluator_watching_for: Physical inspection before calibration — candidate does not skip straight to the protractor.
Free movement verified through the full mechanical range. Any binding reported, not glossed over.
Correct fixture for the vane type. Mechanical protractor for a vane; pressure fixture for smart AoA.
Readings taken at every specified angle, not just the easy ones.
Out-of-tolerance results drive a root-cause investigation, not a 'just adjust until it passes' response.
Stall-warning system functional check performed after re-rig.
common_mistakes: Ignoring the physical inspection and going straight to calibration. A contaminated or slightly bent vane calibrates out on the bench but fails in flight (XL Airways 888T).
Using an uncalibrated protractor or fixture. If the tool is wrong by 0.5°, the calibration is wrong by 0.5°.
Adjusting the rigging to force one angle into tolerance and accepting the overall calibration without re-verifying all points.

Not verifying the stall-warning trigger point after re-rig. AoA at stick-shaker is a specific value from the aircraft's flight manual; a re-rigged vane that is within AoA tolerance may still fire the shaker at the wrong speed.
Documenting calibration without the raw data. The logbook entry must show angle-by-angle error numbers, not just 'AoA calibration complete'.

429.title: ADC Output Verification (ARINC 429)
step_by_step: Review the aircraft wiring manual and ADC ICD (Interface Control Document) for the exact ARINC 429 labels, speed (low-speed 12.5 kbps or high-speed 100 kbps), and update rates.
Connect a calibrated ARINC 429 bus analyzer (AIT PBA.pro, DDC DD-429-USB, Excalibur MX-429, or equivalent) to the ADC output bus at the test port specified by the AMM.
Configure the analyzer: correct speed, correct label filter (203, 204, 205, 206, 210, 211, 212, 221 for typical air data), and a capture buffer of 60 seconds to get a statistically meaningful sample.
Apply known pitot-static inputs from a test set: for example, set altitude 10,000 ft, CAS 250 kt, Mach nominal. Let the system stabilize for 30 seconds.
Verify each label is present in the capture, at the expected update rate (typically 16–20 Hz; some labels may transmit at 8 Hz). A missing label means the ADC is not computing that parameter.

Decode each label: label 203 altitude in feet (BCD), label 205 Mach, label 206 CAS in knots, label 210 TAS in knots, label 211 TAT in °C, label 212 SAT in °C, label 221 AoA in degrees. Compare against the test set reference.

Check the SSM (Sign-Status Matrix, bits 30–31) on each label. 'Normal Operation' (11) is accept. 'No Computed Data' (01) means the ADC cannot compute this parameter — often indicates a sensor or input fault. 'Functional Test' (10) should only appear during IBIT. 'Failure Warning' (00) is a reject.

Verify parity: ARINC 429 uses odd parity on bit 32. The analyzer flags parity errors; more than a few per minute indicates a bus signal-integrity problem (loose connector, degraded shield, wrong termination).
Repeat at a second flight condition (e.g., 30,000 ft, Mach 0.75) to verify performance across the envelope.
Save the bus capture as part of the maintenance record. Print or export the decoded label values with timestamps for the logbook attachment.
evaluator_watching_for: Correct analyzer speed configured for the bus under test. Wrong speed = no data.
Full set of expected labels verified, not just one or two.
SSM decoded on every label. A label with a valid value but SSM = 'No Computed Data' is NOT a good label.
Test set inputs verified against reported outputs at each flight condition.
Parity and bus-integrity checked, not ignored.
Capture saved for the record.
common_mistakes: Running the analyzer at 100 kbps when the bus is 12.5 kbps (or vice versa). No labels appear; technician declares the ADC dead.
Looking at only label 203 altitude because that is the easy one, and ignoring the Mach/CAS/TAS/TAT/AoA labels. A partially failed ADC passes.
Ignoring SSM. A 'No Computed Data' altitude with a plausible value can mask an internal fault that invalidates the number.
Not applying real test-set inputs. Simply confirming labels are 'present' with stale data from the last flight is not a verification.
Forgetting parity. A slowly degrading cable with 1% parity errors is an impending ADC drop-off; ignoring it accepts an aircraft that will go no-go in three weeks.

synthesized_oral_board: Q1: Walk me through a pitot-static leak check. What are the acceptance criteria, and what are the three most likely causes of a failure?
Q2: A smart probe reports valid altitude on ARINC 429 but the SSM on the airspeed label reads 'No Computed Data.' What does that tell you, and how do you troubleshoot?
Q3: Explain why TAT reads higher than SAT in flight, by how much at Mach 0.8, and why the ADC must be able to correct for it.

Q4: An aircraft has just had the left pitot probe replaced. The RVSM-approved aircraft is scheduled for a trip tonight. What is the regulatory and maintenance status, and what do you need to do before the aircraft can return to RVSM airspace?
Q5: Tell me what happened on Air France 447 from an avionics-systems standpoint, and what a modern ADC architecture does differently to prevent a repeat.

oral_board_answers.Q1: A pitot-static leak check verifies the pneumatic integrity of both the pitot and static systems by pressurizing them to simulated flight conditions and measuring leak rate over 1 minute. Acceptance per 14 CFR 91.411 and typical AMM specs: static system leaks at no more than 100 feet of altitude drop in 1 minute when tested at 25,000 feet altitude, and pitot system leaks at no more than 5 knots in 1 minute at 100 knots indicated. On RVSM aircraft the tolerances are tighter. The three most common failure causes are: (1) a deteriorated O-ring or gasket at a fitting — often at a drain trap or bulkhead penetration where vibration and temperature cycling have aged the seal; (2) a cracked plastic or aluminum static line from someone bending it during an earlier maintenance action — this is especially common behind instrument panels where the line was disturbed during a radio installation; (3) a loose or damaged static port bonding, where the seal between the port flange and the skin has relaxed. I would verify the adapter seal first (a common false fail), pressurize slowly, and if leakage exceeds spec, use a soap-bubble solution to localize the leak before tearing anything apart.
oral_board_answers.Q2: The combination of valid altitude with 'No Computed Data' on airspeed tells me the ADC is still seeing valid static pressure (so the static side is intact) but it cannot compute airspeed — which depends on pitot-minus-static. This almost always points to a pitot-side problem. My troubleshooting sequence: first, visually inspect the pitot probe for ice, insect nests, covers left on after wash, or damage. Second, verify pitot heat is working — measure heater current at the breaker and check for the expected 5–15 A draw; a cold pitot at altitude ices over fast. Third, check for water in the pitot line at the drain trap — I drain it and see if any fluid comes out. Fourth, if the probe is a smart probe, I pull its BITE history; an internal pressure-transducer fault shows up there as a specific fault code. Fifth, I run a pneumatic test on just the pitot side with a known input pressure — if the ADC computes a correct airspeed with the test set but not from the probe, the probe is bad. If the ADC will not compute even with a known pitot input, the ADC is bad. I do NOT clear the 'No Computed Data' code and fly the aircraft just because the cockpit display may not show the flag dramatically. 'No Computed Data' means the airspeed is not valid — the autopilot and autothrottle should also be flagged.
oral_board_answers.Q3: TAT reads higher than SAT because of compressibility heating — also called ram-rise or adiabatic heating. Air moving at high speed relative to the aircraft is decelerated to near-zero velocity at the probe's sensing element, and the kinetic energy of that airflow converts to thermal energy. The equation is TAT = SAT × (1 + 0.2 × M² × Kr), where M is Mach and Kr is the recovery factor (how much of theoretical ram-rise the specific probe actually recovers — typically 0.95 to 1.00 for a good probe). At Mach 0.8: 0.2 × 0.8² × 1.0 = 0.128, so TAT is SAT × 1.128. If SAT at cruise is −55 °C, which is 218 K, then TAT is 218 × 1.128 = 246 K, which is −27 °C. That is a 28 °C difference; on a warmer day at Mach 0.8 the difference approaches 40 °C. The ADC must correct for this in both directions: the TAT probe reports the true total air temperature, but what the pilot wants to display is SAT (for density altitude and performance planning) and what the ADC needs to compute TAS (which uses SAT-based density). So the ADC receives Mach from the pitot-static solution, receives TAT from the TAT probe, and computes SAT = TAT / (1 + 0.2 × M² × Kr). If Mach is wrong — say because a blocked pitot port corrupts qc — then SAT is wrong, density altitude is wrong, and TAS is wrong, all in one propagating chain. This is why you never treat the TAT probe as standalone; it is only useful in combination with a good Mach solution.
oral_board_answers.Q4: The aircraft is out of RVSM approval the moment maintenance touches the pitot-static system or any primary air data component. That is not my opinion; it is the RVSM maintenance manual and AC 91-85. Before the aircraft can legally fly in RVSM airspace (FL290 to FL410) it must complete a full 91.411 static-system test with RVSM-grade tolerances — that is stricter than a standard 24-month test. The pitot-probe replacement specifically: I verify the correct part number and serial number for that position, install per AMM torque and sealant spec, perform a pneumatic leak check on the pitot system, run an ADC functional test to verify the new probe feeds good data to the ADC, and then run the RVSM static-system test. If the ADC itself was not touched, I still check the pitot-static integrity end to end. I document in the logbook: 'Pitot probe P/N ___ S/N ___ replaced in LH position. Pneumatic leak check passed. RVSM altimetry test complete per AC 91-85 on [date]. Aircraft returned to RVSM status.' If any test fails, or if I cannot complete the RVSM test before the trip, the aircraft goes in the logbook as 'Not approved for RVSM. Operations limited to below FL290 or with ATC coordination.' I do not bend that rule no matter who is in the cockpit or the schedule pressure, because the whole point of RVSM is that the system is trustworthy at 65-foot accuracy and my signature is what certifies it.
oral_board_answers.Q5: Air France 447 was an Airbus A330 over the Atlantic at night in June 2009. The aircraft had three pitot probes of the same part number (Thales AA), and all three iced up within seconds of entering a high-altitude convective cell where supercooled ice crystals exceeded the heater's melting capacity. The ADCs lost valid airspeed on all three inputs simultaneously. Because the three ADCs used three probes of the same part with the same weakness, the failure was common-mode — no voting scheme could rescue it. The autopilot disconnected (the A330 cannot fly in normal law without valid airspeed), the aircraft dropped from normal law to alternate law, and the pilots — without the envelope protections they had trained with — mishandled the manual flying at altitude and stalled the aircraft from 37,000 feet into the ocean. All 228 aboard died. From an avionics perspective the lessons are: (1) probe heating matters — Thales AA has since been replaced fleet-wide with the Thales BA or Goodrich probe with higher heater power and different internal aerodynamics. (2) Common-mode failures are the ones that kill — you want your triple redundancy to be genuine redundancy, meaning different probe part numbers, different installation positions, and different heating regulators. (3) ADC architectures now include more aggressive probe-disagreement voting, and the newest systems (A350 with MFP) can cross-check pitot-derived speed against GPS-derived groundspeed plus wind and flag an unreliable-airspeed condition faster than the human can. (4) The BEA report led to new ICAO standards on pitot-probe icing certification — TSO-C16a was revised, and simulator training for unreliable-airspeed procedures became mandatory for airline crews. The avionics technician's takeaway: heat current measurement, element resistance, and drainage are not optional on any inspection, ever.

background_theory.why_MFP_replaces_discrete_probes: The multi-function probe consolidates pitot, static, AoA, and TAT into one externally mounted unit with an embedded processor. The motivation is three-fold: weight, reliability, and data quality. A traditional pitot-static installation requires 4–6 pneumatic lines running 20–40 feet through the airframe, each line needing drain traps, heat insulation, and periodic pressure-checks; that is hundreds of pounds of aluminum and brass plus the leak-path maintenance burden. An MFP replaces the pneumatic tree with a single ARINC 429 twisted-shielded pair. Reliability improves because every pneumatic joint eliminated is a potential leak eliminated; the dispatch MEL for a smart probe installation has fewer common single-point failures than a pneumatic-plumbed equivalent. Data quality improves because the analog-to-digital conversion happens at the probe, so pneumatic lag, water condensation, and line volume are removed from the signal chain. BUT — and this is the engineer's uncomfortable counter — integrating pitot + static + AoA into one box creates a new kind of common-mode failure: a single MFP hardware failure (cracked transducer, bad ARINC 429 driver, processor halt) takes out three parameters at once, not one. The mitigation is architectural: the Airbus A350 installs three MFPs on three different sides of the fuselage, each wired to an independent ADC, and the FMS votes on the three solutions to reject any single MFP's bad output. That is genuine redundancy. It is not enough to have one MFP with good BITE; you need three independent sensors of a failure-independent design to make MFP architecture safer than the pneumatic tree it replaced. Every CAET technician should be able to look at an MFP installation and recognize whether it is truly redundant or merely consolidated.
background_theory.how_pitot_heat_is_regulated_for_icing: A pitot heater is a resistive element embedded in the probe body and sometimes around the probe tip. Power is regulated by a pitot heat control unit (PHCU) that manages two objectives in tension: prevent ice accumulation under the worst icing conditions the aircraft can encounter, while not cooking the probe on the ground with no cooling airflow. The icing side is ruthless — supercooled water droplets at −20 °C and appendix-O mixed-phase ice can deposit on an unheated probe fast enough to block it in 30 seconds, and Appendix D (the historical icing envelope) is known to under-represent real-world high-altitude ice crystal exposure. So the heater must dissipate enough power to evaporate accumulating water: typical 300–500 W per probe, at 28 V DC drawing 10–15 A. On the ground, applying 400 W to a probe with no airflow raises the internal temperature above 300 °C in under a minute; the PHCU detects ground/air state through weight-on-wheels or airspeed and reduces to a maintenance-level power (around 100 W) during ground ops. The PHCU also monitors heater current — a drop to zero means an open heater element, a drop to nominal-minus-20% means a degraded element with internal strand breaks. Both illuminate a cockpit caution. Smart probes add another layer: the internal processor monitors probe tip temperature directly with an embedded thermistor and will modulate heat via pulse-width modulation to hold the tip in a narrow temperature band, regardless of external conditions. The avionics technician verifies heat current draw at cold element resistance (the 2 Ω ± 20% test in Task 4), confirms the PHCU is switching correctly between ground and flight modes, and catches any partial-element degradation before it becomes a full failure during flight in icing.
background_theory.why_TAT_needs_correction_for_Mach: Air temperature measurement is deceptively hard at transonic speed. A thermometer in a moving airstream does not read the temperature of the air; it reads the temperature of air that has been decelerated to near-zero velocity at the sensing element. That deceleration converts kinetic energy to thermal energy — adiabatic ram-rise — and the probe sees a temperature higher than the true static air temperature. The fundamental thermodynamic relation is Tt / Ts = 1 + (γ−1)/2 × M², where γ = 1.4 for air, giving Tt = Ts × (1 + 0.2 × M²) for a probe that captures 100 percent of the ram-rise. Real probes never capture 100 percent because there is always some heat loss from the sensing element to the probe body and airstream; the recovery factor Kr models that loss and is determined empirically for each probe design — typically 0.95 to 1.00 for modern TAT probes. At Mach 0.8 the ram-rise is 12.8 percent of absolute temperature; for SAT = 218 K (−55 °C) at FL350, TAT = 246 K (−27 °C), a 28 °C offset. The ADC must run this correction continuously to give the pilot the SAT reading they need for density altitude, performance charts, and icing-envelope awareness. The correction depends on Mach, and Mach depends on pitot-static inputs, and pitot-static inputs can be wrong — so a corrupt Mach value feeds a corrupt SAT value, which feeds a corrupt TAS value, which the FMS uses for wind calculation, fuel burn prediction, and top-of-descent computation. One static-port blockage propagates into half a dozen downstream errors. This is why smart probe architectures feed the same Mach solution into every calculation, and why the ADC flags SSM = 'No Computed Data' on every derived parameter (not just the primary) when the primary input goes bad. The job of the avionics technician is to verify the integrity of the primary measurements (pitot, static, TAT) because every downstream air-data number the pilot sees is a function of those three.
